hook: A sunken Japanese garden carved into an abandoned limestone quarry, with a 60-foot waterfall and koi ponds.
facts: Built in a former limestone quarry on land donated to the city in 1899 by George Washington Brackenridge; the sunken garden was developed in the early 20th century.
In 1919 the city invited Japanese-American artist Kimi Eizo Jingu to live at the garden; he and wife Miyoshi raised eight children there and opened the Bamboo Room tea house in 1926.
The Jingu family was evicted in 1942 amid anti-Japanese sentiment during WWII, after which the site was renamed the 'Chinese Tea Garden' for decades.
Mayor Henry Cisneros restored the original 'Japanese Tea Garden' name in a 1984 ceremony attended by Jingu's children and Japanese government representatives.
A $1.6 million restoration of the ponds, waterfall and filtration system led to a public re-opening on March 8, 2008, with Jingu family members present.
The entrance torii-style gate was built by Mexican-born artist Dionicio Rodriguez in faux bois (cement imitating wood).
w: In an old limestone quarry, a sunken garden of koi ponds, stone paths and a waterfall took shape in the 1910s, run for years by the Jingu family. Wartime anti-Japanese feeling got them evicted and the place renamed 'Chinese Tea Garden.' A Dionicio Rodriguez faux bois gate guards the entrance. Free, lush and quietly storied.

hook: Dr. Aureliano Urrutia's lost Mexican garden of art, scattered with faux bois sculptures and Talavera-tiled gates.
facts: Established around 1921 when Mexican-emigre physician Dr. Aureliano Urrutia bought 15 acres near East Hildebrand Avenue and Broadway, inspired by his birthplace of Xochimilco.
Contains at least ten faux bois ('imitation wood') and faux stone works by artist Dionicio Rodriguez, including the entry gate, a covered bench and a grotto.
The East Hildebrand gates are flanked by towers clad in panels of brightly colored Mexican Talavera ceramic tile.
Owned by the City of San Antonio since 2006; listed on the National Register of Historic Places and designated a State Archaeological Landmark by the Texas Historical Commission.
A pedestrian bridge across the San Antonio River, completed in 2008, connects Miraflores to Brackenridge Park.
After long neglect the city undertook a major restoration, with the historic garden opening to public view in the 2020s.
w: Dr Aureliano Urrutia, a surgeon who fled the Mexican Revolution, built this private sculpture garden in the 1920s as a memorial to his homeland, studding it with tilework, statues and faux bois 'wood' by Dionicio Rodriguez. Long locked away and decaying, it passed to the city in 2006. A lost Eden along the river, slowly coming back.

n: Hot Wells Hotel and Spa Ruins
hook: Stabilized ruins of a Victorian sulfur-springs resort that once drew bathers, film studios and high society.
facts: The story began in 1892 when a well drilled for the Southwestern Lunatic Asylum struck hot, sulfurous water near the San Antonio River, emerging at about 104 degrees F.
Brewer Otto Koehler expanded the property; the three-story Hot Wells Hotel had about 80 rooms by 1902 and was enlarged to roughly 190 rooms by 1908.
Fires repeatedly struck the site, including the destruction of the original bathhouse in 1894 and a devastating 1925 hotel fire.
Developer James Lifshutz donated the ruins and surrounding land to Bexar County in 2015, which allocated about $4 million to stabilize them.
The site reopened as Hot Wells of Bexar County, the county's first historic-cultural park, on April 30, 2019, at 5503 S. Presa St.
The original well has been capped, but visitors can walk among the reinforced hotel and bathhouse ruins.
w: Brick arches and roofless walls are all that survive of Hot Wells, a glamorous Victorian resort built around sulphur hot springs that drew Hollywood stars and a film studio. Fire, Prohibition and decline did it in, leaving haunting ruins now kept as a county park. Wander the bones of a forgotten spa. Faded grandeur on the south side.

hook: A 1917 bridge on the old Applewhite Road alignment, the focus of San Antonio's enduring 'Donkey Lady' ghost legend.
facts: The bridge was built in 1917 and carried the old alignment of Applewhite Road across the Medina River, roughly four miles north of Loop 1604 on the far South Side.
It was closed to vehicles in 2005 when the new Applewhite Road bridge opened.
The old span became part of the Medina River section of the Howard W. Peak Greenway Trails and is reachable on foot or by bike.
Despite the popular 'Elm Creek' framing of the legend, the bridge itself spans the Medina River.
legend: Local lore calls it 'Donkey Lady Bridge' after a woman said to have been horribly disfigured in a fire, or whose soul merged with a drowned donkey, leaving a half-human, half-donkey creature.
The legend dates to at least the 1950s and possibly earlier; honking your horn on the bridge is said to summon her.
Drivers have claimed a creature dropped onto their vehicle, screamed and clawed at the windows.
w: A lonely old bridge over the Medina River carries one of San Antonio's favourite scary stories: the Donkey Lady, a woman said to have been burned in a fire, who haunts the span as a braying half-human shape. Teenagers still dare each other to park here at midnight. The bridge is real; the rest is up to you. Windows down, if you dare.

hook: A real creek whose name loosely translates the 'La Llorona' legend, crossed by Interstate 10 east of San Antonio.
facts: Woman Hollering Creek is a creek in Bexar County that crosses Interstate 10 between Seguin and San Antonio, near Schertz, between exits 591 and 593.
It runs from the sand flats south of Randolph Air Force Base's golf course and ends about 18 miles from downtown San Antonio at Martinez Creek near St. Hedwig.
Maps from the 1830s label the stream 'Arroyo de la Llorona', and the English name is probably a loose translation of the Spanish La Llorona ('the weeping woman').
The creek lends its name to Sandra Cisneros's 1991 short-story collection 'Woman Hollering Creek and Other Stories'.
I-10 Exit 591 (FM 1518) at Schertz sits at about 29.488 N, -98.224 W; the creek crosses the freeway just east of there.
legend: The creek is tied to the La Llorona folk legend of a weeping woman who drowned her children and wanders waterways at night searching for them.
w: An ordinary creek that Interstate 10 crosses east of the city carries an extraordinary name — Woman Hollering Creek, an old rendering of 'Arroyo de la Llorona,' the weeping woman who drowned her children and wanders the water. Sandra Cisneros borrowed it for a story. Blink and you'll miss the sign. A roadside ditch, thick with myth.

hook: A South Side railroad crossing where parked cars seem to roll uphill off the tracks - blamed on ghost children.
facts: The crossing sits at the intersection of Villamain Road and Shane Road (about 2902 Shane Rd, 78223), near the San Juan Mission on the South Side.
Cars left in neutral on the tracks appear to roll 'uphill' off them; surveyors found the road is actually on a roughly two-degree downhill grade, a gravity illusion.
Many surrounding streets are named after children (e.g., Cindy, Bobbie), feeding the legend, though the names predate it.
Union Pacific reconstructed the crossing in 2018-2019, adding a second track and altering the elevation in ways expected to end the rolling phenomenon.
legend: Local lore says a school bus stalled on the tracks and was struck by a train, killing the children aboard in the 1930s-1940s.
Visitors say the ghost children push stopped cars off the tracks to safety, sometimes leaving small handprints (often revealed with baby powder on the bumper).
Investigators note no such San Antonio crash is documented; a similar fatal bus-train crash actually occurred in 1938 in Utah.
w: At a quiet railroad crossing on the south side, leave your car in neutral and it seems to roll uphill off the tracks — the work, locals insist, of ghost children who died here in a bus crash. The real tragedy happened in Utah; the slope only fakes the climb. Dust the trunk with powder for tiny handprints. Believe what you like.

hook: A city park named for San Antonio's eccentric millionaire Yeti-and-Nessie hunter, with a metal Loch Ness Monster statue rising from its quarry pond.
facts: The 63-acre park at 7400 TX-151 Access Rd is named for Thomas Baker 'Tom' Slick Jr. (1916-1962), the San Antonio oilman, scientist and cryptozoologist who funded 1950s-60s expeditions for the Yeti, Bigfoot and the Loch Ness Monster.

A metal sculpture titled 'Nessie' by San Antonio artist Elizabeth Carrington sits partly submerged in the park's former-quarry pond as a nod to Slick's cryptozoology; the figure appears and disappears as the water level rises and falls.
Slick first searched for the Loch Ness Monster as a Yale student in 1937, before later mounting Himalayan Yeti expeditions and Pacific-Northwest Bigfoot hunts.

The park is open daily (roughly 5 a.m.-11 p.m.) and includes dog parks, trails, sports courts, fitness equipment and covered playscapes, operated by the City of San Antonio Parks & Recreation department.
legend: Local lore treats the submerged Nessie figure as the park's 'real' Loch Ness Monster, said to surface only when the drought-prone quarry pond is full.
w: A suburban park named for Tom Slick, the San Antonio oil heir who funded real expeditions to hunt the Yeti, Bigfoot and the Loch Ness Monster in the 1950s. Fittingly, a metal Nessie sculpture rises from the park's quarry pond, surfacing and vanishing with the water level. A monster in the suburbs, honouring a monster-hunter.

a: Garden Ridge / southern Comal County (north of San Antonio)
hook: The summer home of the world's largest bat colony - up to 20 million Mexican free-tailed bats spiraling out of a sinkhole at dusk.
facts: Bracken Cave, in southern Comal County just north of San Antonio near Garden Ridge, hosts an estimated 15-20 million Mexican free-tailed bats from roughly March to October, the largest known concentration of bats (and of mammals) anywhere.
The cave and about 1,521 acres of surrounding land are owned and protected by Bat Conservation International, headquartered in Austin, Texas.
The crescent-shaped cave mouth, about 100 feet (30 m) wide, lies at the bottom of a sinkhole formed when the cave roof collapsed.
General access to the cave is restricted to protect the colony; Bat Conservation International runs members-only and ticketed evening 'bat flight' viewings to watch the emergence.
Wikipedia gives the cave's coordinates as 29.68715 N, 98.352606 W, placing it inside the catalogue bbox (lng -98.80 to -98.15, lat 29.15 to 29.80).
w: Each summer evening, fifteen million Mexican free-tailed bats pour out of this cave north of the city in a roaring spiral — the largest bat colony on Earth. Bat Conservation International protects the site, opening it only to ticketed members. Owls and hawks pick off the stragglers overhead. Nature at a scale that defies belief.

n: The Quadrangle at Fort Sam Houston
a: Fort Sam Houston (JBSA), northeast San Antonio
hook: An 1876 windowless military quadrangle that briefly held Geronimo - now an open courtyard roamed by tame deer, peacocks and rabbits.
facts: Built in 1876 as a U.S. Army Quartermaster Depot, the Quadrangle (1400 E. Grayson St.) is the oldest structure at Fort Sam Houston; its 90-foot limestone tower of 1876-78 was first a water/watch tower and was converted to a clock tower in 1882.
In 1886 the Apache leader Geronimo, with about 32 other Apache men, women and children, was held prisoner inside the Quadrangle for roughly six weeks following his surrender.

The enclosed grassy courtyard is home to free-roaming deer, peacocks and other birds, and rabbits, tame enough to approach visitors; the animals actually pre-date Geronimo and the reason for introducing them is not documented.

The Quadrangle sits on the active Joint Base San Antonio-Fort Sam Houston; visitors without a military ID/DoD credential generally need to clear a visitor-pass/background check at a base gate to enter.
legend: A popular but unverified story claims the deer were placed in the courtyard so the imprisoned Geronimo and his Apaches could hunt or feel 'more at home' after refusing Army food; historians note the deer predate Geronimo's stay.
w: A handsome 1876 military quadrangle at Fort Sam Houston, ranged around a clock tower and lawns where deer, peacocks and rabbits wander freely among visitors. The Apache leader Geronimo was briefly held here in 1886. It sits on an active Army base, so bring ID for the gate. A petting-zoo of a parade ground, with a hard history.

a: Fort Sam Houston (JBSA), northeast San Antonio
hook: A marble grave with four embedded horseshoes for an Army cavalry horse who got a full military funeral with Taps.
facts: Pat, a brown thoroughbred foaled in 1908, came to Fort Sam Houston in 1912 and pulled guns for the 3rd and 12th Field Artillery; he was used for training drills and became a favorite of the troops.

When the 12th Field Artillery mechanized in 1938, soldiers petitioned to retire Pat rather than turn him in; the War Department approved, and he lived out his years with his own paddock, groom and a blanket marked with service stripes.
Pat died in 1953 at age 45 and was given a military funeral attended by more than 100 mourners, with a eulogy and the playing of Taps.

His marble grave stands near a post entrance at North Pine and Cunningham Avenue on Fort Sam Houston, with four horseshoes set in concrete over the burial site and a portrait of Pat etched on the headstone (Find a Grave memorial ID 13415440).
The grave is on the active Joint Base San Antonio-Fort Sam Houston, so non-DoD visitors typically need a base visitor pass / ID check to reach it.
w: On the grounds of Fort Sam Houston lies Pat, a cavalry horse so beloved that when he died in 1953 the Army gave him a full military funeral — said to be the only horse ever so honoured. A small marble headstone marks the grave of a soldier who happened to be a horse. Bring ID for the base. A final salute, four hooves and all.

n: U.S. Army Medical Department Museum (AMEDD Museum)
a: Fort Sam Houston (JBSA), northeast San Antonio
hook: A military-medicine museum of battlefield ambulances, field hospitals and POW artifacts on the Army's medical home base.
facts: The U.S. Army Medical Department (AMEDD) Museum is at 2310 Stanley Road, Building 1046, Fort Sam Houston, San Antonio, TX 78234 - the 'Home of Army Medicine.'

Collections cover the history of the Army Medical Department, including medical evacuation vehicles and aircraft, field surgical and dental equipment, uniforms, and prisoner-of-war / medical-history artifacts.

The museum is free to visit, but sits on active Joint Base San Antonio-Fort Sam Houston; the nearest gate is the MP checkpoint at Stanley and Harry Wurzbach Roads (off I-410), and all occupants of a vehicle must show photo ID to enter the base.
It is run as part of the AMEDD Center of History & Heritage and is supported by the AMEDD Museum Foundation.
w: The U.S. Army's medical history fills this Fort Sam Houston museum: Civil War surgical kits, field ambulances, helicopters and the grisly tools of battlefield medicine. It traces how war drove advances that saved civilian lives too. On an active base, so carry ID. Where the hard science of patching soldiers is laid bare.

hook: Billed as America's oldest children's amusement park, with a hand-carved 1918 carousel - relocated in 2019 to the San Antonio Zoo.
facts: Kiddie Park, established in 1925, is recognized as one of (often billed as) America's oldest children's amusement parks, with vintage kid-scale rides including a Ferris wheel and carousel.
Its centerpiece is a hand-carved wooden carousel built in 1918 by the Herschell-Spillman Company, which has operated in San Antonio since the 1930s.

In the summer of 2019 the San Antonio Zoo took over Kiddie Park and moved it from its longtime Broadway / East Mulberry Avenue site (near Brackenridge Park) to the zoo grounds; the original hand-painted sign was preserved and moved as well.
The relocated park now operates at 3903 N. St. Mary's Street in Brackenridge Park, at the San Antonio Zoo, and is open to the public (typically Friday-Sunday).
w: Billed as one of America's oldest children's amusement parks, Kiddie Park has spun its gentle rides since 1925, centred on a 1918 Herschell-Spillman carousel that grown-ups may ride too. It moved to the San Antonio Zoo in 2019, vintage sign and all. Tiny ferris wheel, painted ponies. A century of childhood, still turning.

hook: An 1881 saloon crammed floor-to-ceiling with horns, taxidermy and oddities, where the founder once traded drinks for antlers.
facts: The Buckhorn Saloon was founded in 1881 by Albert Friedrich (1864-1928), who began bartering free drinks and beer for sets of horns and antlers brought in by patrons, building the original 'Buckhorn Hall of Horns'.
It now operates as the Buckhorn Saloon & Museum at 318 E. Houston Street in downtown San Antonio, combining a working saloon with the museum and the Texas Ranger Museum.
The collection includes mounted wildlife representing over 520 species, among them a 78-point buck, a 1,056-pound black marlin and an eight-foot-spread longhorn.
Exhibits also feature Texas Ranger artifacts, a wax museum of Texas history and a recreated turn-of-the-century San Antonio streetscape ('Ranger Town').
The saloon moved locations several times over its history before settling at its current Houston Street site, and remains a paid-admission tourist attraction open to the public today.
legend: The museum has long promoted itself as displaying 'the first gorilla ever on public display in the United States', a claim repeated in its own marketing.
w: Since 1881 this downtown saloon has covered its walls in horns and taxidermy traded for drinks, a cabinet of curiosities thousands of mounts deep. Two-headed calves and a fish-scale chandelier share the rooms with a Texas Ranger museum. Order a beer beneath a thousand glass eyes. Texas tall tales, stuffed and mounted.

hook: A free riverside courtyard garden of bronze cowboys, longhorns and cougars beside the Briscoe Western Art Museum.
facts: The McNutt Sculpture Garden is the outdoor courtyard of the Briscoe Western Art Museum at 210 W. Market Street, along the San Antonio River Walk in the former 1930s San Antonio Public Library building.
The garden is named in honor of philanthropist Amy Shelton McNutt.
It opened with the Briscoe Western Art Museum, which debuted on October 26, 2013, and the garden was later expanded with additional sculptures around the Jack Guenther Pavilion.
The garden displays bronze works by leading Western artists depicting cowboys, American Indians and animals including horses, longhorns, cougars and snakes.
It is free to enter during museum hours, with outdoor tables and seating, and can be reached via a gate off the River Walk side of the museum campus.
w: Tucked beside the Briscoe Western Art Museum on the River Walk, this small courtyard garden gathers bronze cowboys, vaqueros and longhorns among palms and a quiet fountain. Free to wander, it makes a shaded pause between galleries and the river. Western myth, cast in bronze among the greenery. An easy hidden gem off the bustle.

hook: A bronze Berlin bear gifted by West Berlin for HemisFair '68, still standing guard by the Victorian Halff House.
facts: The Berlin Bear is a bronze sculpture of a bear, the heraldic emblem of Berlin, given by West Berlin to San Antonio for the 1968 World's Fair, HemisFair '68.
It was created by artist Guss W. Fussel and was originally exhibited among more than 100 artworks from some 30 nations at the fair.
The bear now stands in Hemisfair park in front of the historic Halff House, a Victorian-era residence that predates the fairgrounds.
The sculpture is catalogued in the Smithsonian American Art Inventory of public sculpture.
It remains a freely visitable outdoor monument within the redeveloped Hemisfair park downtown.
w: A stout painted bear stands in Hemisfair Park, a gift from West Berlin for the 1968 HemisFair world's fair — the bear being Berlin's heraldic symbol, a token of cold-war friendship. Few passers-by know why a German city's mascot guards a San Antonio lawn. Pat its paw and wonder. A small diplomatic curiosity, hiding in plain sight.

hook: A one-acre private-public memorial garden a philanthropist built for her late son, with journal-inscribed limestone benches and a hidden LED constellation.
facts: Chris Park is a roughly one-acre public garden created by philanthropist and artist Linda Pace in memory of her son, Christopher 'Chris' Goldsbury, who died on April 10, 1997.
It opened in 2005 and is privately maintained by the Linda Pace Foundation (now part of Ruby City) while remaining free and open to the public.
The artist Teresita Fernandez conceived the park's visual experiences, including limestone benches inscribed with passages from Chris's journals.
Features include 'Starfield', tiny blue LED lights embedded in stone that form constellations from Chris's birth date at night, and illuminated glass pavers etched with a modified nursery rhyme.
The park is located on Camp Street in the Southtown / King William area, near the Linda Pace Foundation's exhibition spaces.
w: The art patron Linda Pace built this small green park in Southtown as a living memorial to her son Chris, who died young — dappled light, plantings and quiet corners laden with private meaning. Open to all, it sits beside her Ruby City art museum. A mother's grief, turned into a gift for the city. Sit a while in someone else's love.

n: La Veladora of Our Lady of Guadalupe Mosaic
hook: A monumental votive-candle (veladora) mosaic of the Virgin of Guadalupe, a tiled beacon on the West Side's Guadalupe Theater.
facts: 'La Veladora of Our Lady of Guadalupe' is a monumental mosaic by San Antonio artist Jesse Trevino, completed in 2003 on the facade of the Guadalupe Theater at 1301 Guadalupe Street.
The work depicts the Virgin of Guadalupe rendered as a giant votive candle (veladora) and includes a three-dimensional candle with an eternal flame facing Guadalupe Street as a neighborhood beacon.
It is built from vibrant red and gold ceramic tiles and is often described as among the largest mosaic depictions of the Virgin Mary.
The Guadalupe Theater is operated in connection with the Guadalupe Cultural Arts Center, anchoring the predominantly Mexican-American West Side.

Jesse Trevino completed the first restoration of the mosaic from July 30 to August 2, 2019, financed by the Avenida Guadalupe Association and the Guadalupe Cultural Arts Center, about 17 years after its installation.
The mosaic is a public landmark where visitors come to pray and leave flowers and small votive candles.
w: On the Guadalupe Theater rises a four-storey mosaic of a votive candle, its painted flame flickering over the West Side — 'La Veladora,' said to be one of the world's largest devotional mosaics. Artist Jesse Trevino, who lost a hand in Vietnam, spent years tiling this tribute to Our Lady of Guadalupe. Faith, four storeys tall.

a: North San Antonio (Loop 410 / San Pedro Avenue)
hook: A pair of giant cowboy boots, billed as the world's largest, marking the entrance to North Star Mall off Loop 410.
facts: The giant cowboy boots, titled 'The Giant Justins', were created by Austin artist Bob 'Daddy-O' Wade in 1979, originally for the Washington Project for the Arts in Washington, D.C.
The Rouse Company, owners of North Star Mall, bought the boots for about $20,000, and they were reassembled in San Antonio on January 16, 1980.

The boots stand about 35 feet 3 inches tall, 33 feet 4 inches long and 9 feet wide, weighing around 5,000 pounds each, built from tubular steel sprayed with polyurethane foam (later given a concrete coating).
Guinness World Records recognized them as the largest cowboy boots, a designation often cited as awarded in the mid-2010s.
They stand on the north property line of North Star Mall (7400 San Pedro Avenue) just off the Loop 410 frontage road, and remain a free, publicly viewable roadside landmark.
Artist Bob 'Daddy-O' Wade died in December 2019; staff have said the boots were his favorite among his sculptures.
legend: Wade liked to claim each boot could hold roughly 300,000 gallons of beer, a piece of showman's hyperbole rather than a measured figure.
w: Two cowboy boots the size of a house — thirty-five feet of fibreglass and steel — stand outside North Star Mall. Bob 'Daddy-O' Wade built them in the late 1970s, and after a stint in Washington they clomped home to Texas. Pure roadside swagger, made for selfies. Stand in the shadow of a boot taller than your house.

hook: Keeps the cedar bench an airman named Johnny Cash knife-carved "Johnny Loves Vivian" into in 1951.
facts: Texas natural-history, science and culture museum at 3801 Broadway, on the edge of Brackenridge Park, founded in 1926.
While stationed at nearby Lackland AFB in 1951, future country star Johnny Cash carved "Johnny Loves Vivian" into a large cedar bench on the River Walk for his sweetheart Vivian Liberto.
The bench had been designed by River Walk architect Robert Hugman, so when Liberto returned in 2004 and asked to buy it, the city refused and put it in storage.
After nine years in storage the city donated the bench to the Witte in 2013, where it is displayed at the B. Naylor Morton Research and Collections Center.
The carving is now faint, so exhibit signage points out where the inscription is and translates it for visitors.
The museum tells the story of Texas from prehistory to the present, with dinosaur bones, wildlife dioramas, Texas art and Indigenous artifacts.
w: This Broadway museum of Texas nature and history holds dinosaurs, rock art and frontier stories — but its most romantic exhibit, a humble cedar bench, tells a love story. On it, in 1951, airman Johnny Cash carved 'Johnny Loves Vivian' while stationed here. The bench survived; the marriage did not. Come for the fossils, stay for the bench.

hook: A concrete footbridge so convincingly "wooden" that woodpeckers peck at its bark hunting for insects.
facts: Built around 1925-1926 by Mexican-born artisan Dionicio Rodriguez (1891-1955), it spans an old acequia (irrigation canal) near Lambert Beach off Hildebrand Avenue.
It is entirely reinforced concrete sculpted to imitate an arbor of woven, knotted wooden limbs - Rodriguez's faux bois ("false wood") technique he called el trabajo rustico.
Rodriguez built a metal armature, applied sand-free cement, sculpted it with forks, knives, spoons and twigs, then stained it while wet with chemicals such as copperas, sulfuric acid and lampblack.
Considered his most significant design in the park, the bridge drew the attention of Popular Mechanics magazine in 1927.
It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places on October 22, 2004 (ref. 04001166), part of the "Sculpture by Dionicio Rodriguez in Texas" multiple-property listing.
legend: Visitors and guides report woodpeckers landing on the concrete "bark" of the railings and pecking, fooled into hunting for insects inside.
w: It looks like a rustic log bridge of knotted branches and bark — but knock on it and it rings: every twig is concrete. Dionicio Rodriguez built this faux bois ('false wood') crossing in Brackenridge Park in the 1920s, fooling the eye completely. He guarded his colouring secrets to the grave. Run your hand over the fake bark and marvel.

n: Dionicio Rodriguez Faux Bois Trolley Stop
hook: A bus shelter of "petrified wood" that is really hand-sculpted concrete - the city's most accessible artwork.
facts: Built in 1931 by Dionicio Rodriguez at the corner of Broadway and Patterson Avenue in Alamo Heights (4900 Broadway).
Many passers-by mistake the faux bois shelter for petrified wood, though it is concrete, Rodriguez's signature medium.
It was donated to the City of Alamo Heights by the Alamo Cement Company and has been moved only once - a few feet, when the street was repaved.
It still functions as a public transit/bus stop, making it the most accessible of Rodriguez's San Antonio-area works.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2004 (ref. 04001165) as part of the Dionicio Rodriguez multiple-property listing.
w: At a busy Alamo Heights corner stands a bus shelter masquerading as a gnarled hut, with 'log' benches and a 'thatched' roof — all sculpted concrete by Dionicio Rodriguez in 1931. Commuters still wait inside this faux bois fantasy, few realising it's folk art. Sit on a concrete log and watch the traffic. Art you can catch a bus from.

n: Dionicio Rodriguez Faux Bois Canopied Table & Bench
hook: A concrete picnic table under a fake "thatched" palapa roof - both sculpted entirely from cement.
facts: A faux bois table with two benches, capped by a palapa-style "bundled straw" roof, located just north of Koehler Pavilion in Brackenridge Park.
Made of hand-sculpted reinforced concrete by Dionicio Rodriguez (c. 1925), imitating both rustic wood and a thatched Mexican palapa.
Some of Rodriguez's canopied table-and-bench works were originally installed at Alamo Plaza downtown before being relocated into the park.
It sits among a cluster of Rodriguez faux bois pieces between the Koehler Park entry gates and the San Antonio River, including a palapa-roofed bench near the low-water crossing.
The park's Rodriguez works are part of the National Register-listed "Sculpture by Dionicio Rodriguez in Texas" (2004).
w: Near the old Koehler Pavilion in Brackenridge Park sits a picnic table and benches under a 'thatched' canopy on 'wooden' posts, all sculpted in concrete by Dionicio Rodriguez. Families have eaten lunch on his faux bois furniture for generations. Tap the tabletop: solid stone. The master's quiet trick, in a city park.
